title: Optimized Three-Dimensional Photovoltaic Structures with LLM guided Tree Search
authors: Michael P. Brenner, Lizzie Dorfman, John C. Platt
abstract: We present a case study for how AI coding systems can be used to generate novel scientific hypotheses. We combine a generic coding agent (Google's AntiGravity) with an LLM-driven tree search algorithm (Empirical Research Assistance / ERA) to autonomously generate high-efficiency three-dimensional photovoltaic (3DPV) structures that overcome losses limiting flat solar panels at mid-latitudes. These structures operate by presenting favorable angles to the sun throughout the day, and for illustrative purposes we focus on optimizing performance for a single solar day. Our workflow begins by using AntiGravity to reproduce calculations \cite{bernardi2012solar} showing that 3DPV can have energy densities much higher than stationary flat PV panels. We use these initial designs as the starting point for large scale tree search, where we seek improved solutions and score them for their diurnal yield. The initial tree search leads to nominally more efficient solutions, yet they are caused by algorithmic reward hacking, arising from non-physical design features such as structurally levitating disconnected tiers and exploitations of the discretizations in the optics solver. To counteract this, we develop a workflow where the coding agent iteratively patches the physics engine with constraints to eliminate reward hacking. With reward-hacking eliminated, ERA discovers a series of designs with various constraints and improved performance, including optimal designs with different fixed collector areas, optimizing zenith tracking and avoiding self shadowing.   Combining coding agents with tree search (ERA) provides a powerful platform for scientific discovery, for problems whose solutions can be empirically evaluated with a score function.
summary: This paper demonstrates a novel AI-driven methodology combining coding agents with LLM-guided tree search to optimize 3D photovoltaic structures. By overcoming initial algorithmic flaws, the system discovered high-efficiency designs that significantly improve solar energy capture compared to traditional flat panels. This showcases a powerful platform for scientific discovery in complex, empirically evaluable physical systems.
main_problem: The paper addresses the geometric limitations of flat photovoltaic (PV) panels at mid-latitudes, aiming to maximize solar energy harvesting efficiency.
main_result: The use of LLM-guided tree search (ERA) successfully optimized 3D PV structures, discovering designs like the 'High-Table' and 'Tilted Waffle' that significantly outperform flat panels under various structural constraints.
method: The authors combined a coding agent (AntiGravity) with an LLM-driven tree search algorithm (ERA) to autonomously explore and optimize complex, physically constrained design spaces.
model_or_system: The system is a 3D Photovoltaic (3DPV) structure, modeled using classical thin-film Fresnel optics and simulated over a full solar day using the Solar Position Algorithm (SPA).
key_observables: Total daily solar energy yield (E, in $	ext{mW}\cdot	ext{h}$), peak power, and the structural geometry of the optimized panels.
important_parameters: Solar zenith ($	heta_z$) and azimuth ($\phi_s$), total panel surface area ($A_{total} \le 800 	ext{ m}^2$), and structural constraints (e.g., $3	imes$, $5	imes$, $20	imes$ area limits).
main_assumptions: The initial model assumes only direct irradiance contributes to generation, and the optimization process required iterative patching to eliminate non-physical 'reward hacking' exploits.
figures_summary: Figures compare light curves across various geometries (flat, open cube, High Table, Tilted Sawtooth, etc.), and subsequent figures detail the iterative process of eliminating physical exploits and showing the final optimized structures.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the physical problem (flat panel limitations), details the AI-driven optimization workflow (AntiGravity + ERA), presents the iterative refinement process to ensure physical validity, and concludes by comparing the performance of various optimized 3D geometries under different material constraints.
why_interesting: While the core physics is classical optics, the methodology—using LLMs and automated coding agents to navigate complex, high-dimensional, and physically constrained optimization landscapes—is highly relevant to developing computational tools for quantum materials discovery.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1 | Unified comparative light curves across baseline and optimized geometries under True Legacy physics, including the flat panels and open cube design from Bernardi et al. (2012), as well as the optimal designs we develop in this paper: these include High Table, Tilted Sawtooth, Open South Cavity, and Tilted Waffle.

Figure 3 | Milestone 1 exploit analysis: (a) Algorithmic search progression discovering the design that both increases efficiency but also violates the laws of physics. (b) Optimal state attained by Candidate 104. Physical panels levitate completely unsupported in mid-air (𝑍≈10.0 m) to bypass shading interactions.
Figure 4 | Dispersed light vectors slipping continuously below levitated surface plates.

Figure 5 | Structural optimization renderings under strict 3× footprint area constraints: (a) Evolutionary learning curve showing the optimization breakthroughs leading to the best candidate (Number 188). (b) Structure of the best candidate, a High Table configuration (2011.8 mW·h). (c) An intermediate best candidate (Number 85) with a M-Shape/Tilted Sawtooth configuration (1837.8 mW·h).

Figure 6 | ERA search under the equal-material 5× surface limit: (a) Breakthrough plot. Over iterations the best candidate occurs at candidate 60. (b) Three-dimensional layout of Candidate 60. The primary south-directed open cavity optimizes collection from the southern arc, whereas the internal central partition intercepts lower-elevation lateral rays.
